ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for puri is used as the closest parent. No specific code exists for pizza pani puri fusion in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for pizza pani puri or this fusion variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Pizza Pani Puri consists of approximately 6 filled puri shells, weighing around 180 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Pani Puri (estimated from component analysis): semolina puri, spiced potato filling, pizza sauce, mozzarella cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for pizza pani puri exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: semolina puri shells (~40% by weight, USDA SR Legacy semolina and fried bread data), spiced potato filling (~30%, ICMR-NIN boiled potato with spices), pizza tomato sauce (~15%, USDA SR Legacy tomato sauce), mozzarella cheese (~10%, USDA SR Legacy part-skim mozzarella), and spiced water/chutney (~5%, negligible caloric contribution). Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for this fusion snack.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of Indian fried bread (puri) and potato-based snacks — analogous estimate based on: 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008' (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care, 2008) and University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried wheat bread and potato
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pizza pani puri. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) fried semolina/wheat puri GI ~70 (University of Sydney GI Database, fried wheat bread analogues); (2) potato filling GI ~60–65 (boiled/mashed potato, Atkinson et al. 2008); (3) mozzarella cheese and tomato sauce have negligible GI contribution. Weighted composite estimate yields GI ~65. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 33.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.8 serving size factor) / 100 = approximately 39.3 for a 180 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100g; ~756 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid pizza pani puri due to its high sodium load from pizza sauce, chaat masala, and tamarind water, which can contribute significantly to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycemic index (~65) and high carbohydrate content (~33.5 g per 100g) with moderate glycemic load per serving (~39)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume pizza pani puri with caution or avoid it, as the deep-fried semolina shells and starchy potato filling produce a medium GI and a high glycemic load per serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.4 g per 100g) from cheese, deep-frying oil, and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit pizza pani puri intake due to its saturated fat content from mozzarella cheese and deep-frying, combined with high sodium, which together may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pizza Pani Puri is a creative Indian-Italian fusion street food snack that combines the classic Indian pani puri format with pizza-inspired flavours. The dish consists of crispy deep-fried semolina puri shells — the hollow spherical shells characteristic of traditional pani puri — filled with a mixture of spiced mashed potato, pizza tomato sauce, and melted or crumbled mozzarella cheese, then finished with chaat masala, fresh coriander, and optionally a drizzle of tamarind chutney or spiced mint water. The result is a bite-sized snack that delivers the crunch and spice of Indian street food alongside the tangy, cheesy notes of pizza. In Singapore, this dish is served at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine restaurant at Boat Quay, illustrating the multicultural and experimental food culture of the city-state. Nutritionally, pizza pani puri is a calorie-dense snack (~248 kcal per 100g) with moderate protein (~7.2 g), significant carbohydrates (~33.5 g) from the fried semolina shells and potato filling, and moderate fat (~9.8 g) including saturated fat from cheese. The sodium content is notably high (~420 mg per 100g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium), and the glycemic load per standard serving is high (~39), making it unsuitable for frequent consumption by people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian and halal-friendly but contains gluten and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pizza pani puri?
What is the glycemic index of pani puri?
Is pizza pani puri good for diabetics?
Where can I get pizza pani puri in Singapore?
What are the ingredients in pizza pani puri?
Is pizza pani puri high in sodium?
What is the difference between pani puri and pizza pani puri?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for pizza pani puri exists in any approved primary nutritional database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN data for the constituent ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate based on fried wheat bread and potato entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). The dish is a restaurant-specific fusion creation with no standardised recipe, so actual nutrient values may vary considerably between preparations.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation (USDA SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN do not provide reliable vitamin D values for this food type). 2. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 3. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated values from component analysis where feasible. 4. The food name in the input ('114. Pizza Pani Puri Boom') contains a menu numbering prefix and a promotional suffix ('Boom'); the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Pizza Pani Puri'. 5. The cuisine tag in the input is listed as 'Turkish' (reflecting the restaurant origin — Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine), but the dish itself is an Indian-Italian fusion snack; cuisine has been corrected to 'Fusion (Indian-Italian)'. 6. Multilingual names are provided following Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the dish is served in Singapore. 7. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous fried wheat bread and potato data from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; glycemic load per 180g serving = (65 × 33.5 × 1.8) / 100 ≈ 39.3. 8. Halal status is assessed as likely given the restaurant context (Sultan Palace, a Turkish/Lebanese establishment in Singapore), but formal halal certification has not been independently verified.
